coredata.dc:title: Exploration of pH-responsive carbon dots for detecting nitrite and ascorbic acid
coredata.dc:creator: Gan, Lanlan
Su, Qian
Chen, Zhangbao
Yang, Xiaoming
coredata.dc:description: Detections of carcinogen and vitamins are generally recognized to be meaningful, especially for nitrites (NO2 −) and ascorbic acid (AA). Here blue-fluorescence carbon dots (CDs) with the quantum yield of 11.83% were successfully synthesized by using folic acid and nicotinic acid, and their surfaces were identified to be abundant in the functional groups of amide, carboxyl and hydroxyl. Importantly, the amide on the surfaces of CDs showed the interactions with the nitrites and formed the complex, thus resulting in their quenched fluorescence. To be specific, the fluorescence intensity of CDs proportionally decreased by adding the increased amounts of NO2 − from 8 to 800 μM, and achieved the detection of NO2 − at a detection limit of 21.2 µM. Moreover, further introducing ascorbic acid could prevent CDs with NO2 − to form the complex and recover their fluorescence, and thus providing the new avenue of assaying AA with the generally favorable linear range of 0.1–800 μM, while its related detection limit was 50 µM. In addition, the synthesized CDs exhibited the satisfactory sensitivity toward different pH values, demonstrating their potential of sensing pH. Consequently, the proposed CDs here may provide a new way of monitoring NO2 − and AA in the target samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
pH-responsive
Dual-detection
Nitrite
Ascorbic acid

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optimizing the conditions of CDs detecting NO2 − and AA
Establishing the detections of nitrite and AA
Mechanism of CDs detecting NO2 − and AA
Detection of NO2 − and AA in real sample

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support by Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing ( cstc2018jcyjAX0197 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Scheme of synthesizing CDs and their fluorescent images with introducing NO2 − and AA; (B) Illustration of the mechanism for CDs assaying NO2 − and AA.

(A) Fluorescence and UV–vis spectra of CDs; (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs with different excitations; (C) Fluorescence lifetime spectrum of CDs; (D) HR-TEM images of CDs; (E) Size distribution of CDs by DLS; (F) FTIR spectrum of CDs.
(A) XPS survey of CDs; XPS spectra of C1s (B), O1s (C) and N1s (D); Linear fitting for quantum yield calculation of Quinine sulfate (E) and CDs (F).

(A) Fluorescence spectra of CDs with various pH (2.00–4.08); (B) Images of CDs in acidic conditions with daylight and UV light; (C) Plot of the fluorescence intensity of CDs versus pH; (D) Fluorescence spectra of CDs for different pH (11.21–13.33); (E) Images of CDs in alkaline condition with daylight and UV light; (F) Plot of the fluorescence intensity of CDs versus pH.

(A) Fluorescence spectra and image of CDs without and with two concentrations of NO2 −; (B) Fluorescence spectra of CDs with various concentrations of NO2 −; (C) F/F0-1 versus varied concentrations of NO2 −; (D) Fluorescence spectra and image of CDs with NO2 − by introducing two concentrations of AA; (E) Fluorescence spectra of CDs with NO2 − by adding various concentrations of AA; (F) F/F0-1 versus different concentrations of AA.

(A) UV–vis absorption (a) of NO2 −, fluorescence excitation (b) and emission (c) of CDs; (B) UV–vis absorption of CDs in absence or presence of NO2 −, and CDs with NO2 − by introducing AA; (C) FTIR spectra of CDs without and with NO2 −; (D) FTIR spectra of CDs without and with AA in presence of NO2−; (E) Schematic diagram of CDs interacting with NO2 − and AA.
Recovery of NO2 − in lake water by this method.
Recovery of AA in lake water by this method.

head.ce:title: Exploration of pH-responsive carbon dots for detecting nitrite and ascorbic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, blue-fluorescence carbon dots (CDs) with the quantum yield of 11.83% were successfully synthesized by using folic acid and nicotinic acid. Importantly, the amide on the surfaces of CDs showed the interactions with the nitrites and formed the complex, thus resulting in their quenched fluorescence. Moreover, further introducing ascorbic acid could prevent CDs with NO2 − to form the complex and recover their fluorescence of CDs, and thus providing the new avenue of assaying both NO2 − and ascorbic acid. Besides, the synthesized CDs exhibited the satisfactory sensitivity toward different pH values, demonstrating their potential of sensing pH.

Detections of carcinogen and vitamins are generally recognized to be meaningful, especially for nitrites (NO2 −) and ascorbic acid (AA). Here blue-fluorescence carbon dots (CDs) with the quantum yield of 11.83% were successfully synthesized by using folic acid and nicotinic acid, and their surfaces were identified to be abundant in the functional groups of amide, carboxyl and hydroxyl. Importantly, the amide on the surfaces of CDs showed the interactions with the nitrites and formed the complex, thus resulting in their quenched fluorescence. To be specific, the fluorescence intensity of CDs proportionally decreased by adding the increased amounts of NO2 − from 8 to 800 μM, and achieved the detection of NO2 − at a detection limit of 21.2 µM. Moreover, further introducing ascorbic acid could prevent CDs with NO2 − to form the complex and recover their fluorescence, and thus providing the new avenue of assaying AA with the generally favorable linear range of 0.1–800 μM, while its related detection limit was 50 µM. In addition, the synthesized CDs exhibited the satisfactory sensitivity toward different pH values, demonstrating their potential of sensing pH. Consequently, the proposed CDs here may provide a new way of monitoring NO2 − and AA in the target samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Light-emitting carbon dots (CDs), emerging as one crucial member of fluorescent nanomaterials, have been paid extensive attention owing to their diverse applications in fields of bio-chemical sensing, environmental monitoring, catalysis, cell imaging and beyond. Basically, carbon dots show the advanced properties mainly including tunable luminescence, water solubility, biocompatibility and high thermal stability. Accordingly, multifarious synthetic methods, such as hydrothermal or solvothermal [1–2], microwave-assisted [3], electrochemical [4–5] and laser ablation [6], have been proposed to prepare fluorescent CDs. The further studies also demonstrated that CDs originated from different raw materials exhibit various fluorescence, and people particularly focus on that from environmental-friendly carbon source including fruits, vegetables, plant leaves, waste materials, animal or human derivatives [7], since these CDs are generally biocompatible with low cost [8]. By virtue of their superior fluorescence, diverse CDs have been accordingly utilized to sensing the targets towards various purpose.

Nitrite usually serves as the fertilizer to promote the plant growth in agriculture, which is on the basis of its nitrogen-richness and cooperation with NH4 + and NO3 −. Meanwhile, they also play the role as the preservative in food products, and thus widely appear in daily life and environment. As been known, NO2 − could convert normal hemoglobin to methemoglobin in human blood, and result in the deficiency of transporting oxygen for hemoglobin [9]. Thus, the excessive intake or abuse of nitrites could cause human poisoning, especially shows the serious impact on infants and children. While entering into human bodies through food, nitrites show the interactions with the amides and secondary amines of stomach to produce N-nitrosamines [10], which potentially induce carcinogenesis and hypertension [11]. Hence, World Health Organization and US Environmental Protection Agency recommend the limits of NO2 − as being below 3 mg/L and 1 mg/L for drinking water [12]. Likewise, monitoring the allowable level of nitrite in both food and environment is of great importance.

Ascorbic acid, as an antioxidant, broadly exists in food, animal feed and cosmetics, and plays the role as one indispensable vitamin for human body and associated with the critical processes of human metabolism, which consists of immune function, cell differentiation and iron absorption in intestinal cells [13]. However, the body itself could not produce AA but only from food. Particularly, the healthy concentration of AA in human plasma is identified to range from 0.006 to 0.015 mg/mL [14], and underrated AA may result in the scurvy and immune deficiency, while excessive intake also shows negative effect on human health, even leading to the occurrence of hyperacidity, diarrhea or coronary heart disease [15]. Consequently, paying close attention to the AA level inside of the human body is of great importance.

During the past decades, numerous analytical approaches have been developed for the quantitative determination of nitrite and AA, such as spectrofluorimetric [16], chemiluminescent [17], electrochemical [18], chromatographic [19], capillary electrophoresis [20] and more. Among these, fluorescence-based nanosensors exhibit the advantages of simplicity, sensitivity and high efficiency. As been reported, citric acid and 3-aminoboric acid as the precursors were utilized to synthesize carbon dots through the hydrothermal way for detecting nitrite [21]. Also, the green-fluorescence carbon dots were employed to detect NO2 −, while 4-amino salicylic acid and phosphoric acid served as the reactants [22]. Additionally, it was reported that a switchable fluorescence nanosensor based on the facile carbon dots were applied to determine Fe3+ ion and ascorbic acid [8]. Again, another fluorescent nanoprobe was synthesized by ethylenediamine, glucose and phosphoric acid, and achieved the recognition of chromium (VI) and ascorbic acid via the on-off-on mode [23].

Nicotinic acid, folic acid, glutathione (GSH), aspirin, nacinamide and 4-paracetaminophenol were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China), while sodium nitrite was from Rhawn (Shanghai, China). Meanwhile, metal ions including Ca2+, Ba2+, K+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Na+, Sr2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Pb2+ were obtained from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Likewise, ammonium persulfate (NH4S2O8), sodium chloride (NaCl), sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3), potassium thiocyanate (KSCN), ammonium thiocyanate (NH4CNS), anhydrous sodium carbonate (Na2CO3), disodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4), sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4), anhydrous sodium sulfate (Na2SO4), l-ascorbic acid, glucose, ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA), pyridoxine hydrochloride and melamine were bought from Chron Chemicals Co., Ltd (Chengdu, China). Again, lidocaine hydrochloride and quinine sulphate were acquired from Shanghai Biological Technology Development Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Further, ultrapure water of 18.25 MΩ cm, produced with an Aquapro AWL-0502-P ultrapure water system (Chongqing, China) was utilized for all the experiments, while britton-robinson (BR) buffer served to regulate the pH of solutions during the experimental procedures.

All the fluorescent analyses were achieved through a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). Next, the dynamic light scattering (DLS) data was obtained by using a Zetasizer nano (Malvern, England), and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images were acquired with a TECNAI G2 F20 microscope (Columbia, America) at 200 kV. Simultaneously, we identified the fluorescence lifetime of CDs by HORIBA FL-3 spectrometer (Jobin Yvon) with the excitation of 360 nm. Similarly, ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer (Tokyo, Japan) were utilized to analyze the elements and functional groups. Again, the UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained by introducing a Shimadzu UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan), and a vortex mixer of QL-901 (Haimen, China) was applied to blend the solutions as well as Fangzhong pHS-3C digital pH meter (Chengdu, China) for measuring the pH values of various solutions. Besides, we introduced the centrifuge (TG16-W) of Cence instrument Co., Ltd. (Changsha, China) to separate the mixtures, and employed PiloFD8-4.3V (Charlotte, USA) to freeze-dry the CDs. Furthermore, the electric heating oven of DHG-9140A by Keelrein instrument Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China) was applied for the reactions of preparing CDs, while the water bath of DF-101s from Gongyi experimental instruments factory (Gongyi, China) was for the analytical processes.

During the hydrothermal synthesis of blue-emissive CDs, 0.025 g of nicotinic acid and 0.05 g of folic acid as the precursors were successively added into 6 mL of ultrapure water. After stirring, the mixture was transferred into a 25 mL of Teflon equipped stainless steel autoclave followed by the incubation at 180℃ for 2 h. Subsequently, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature, and the raw products obtained were centrifuged at 9000r/min to remove the larger particles. Then, the further separation of the product away from the unreacted macromolecules was finished by microfiltration membrane. And lastly, the filtered supernatant was subjected to dialysis in ultrapure water for eight hours by using the dialysis bag of MWCO = 500, and the powder of CDs obtained through lyophilization was stored at 4℃ for further use.

During the process of CDs responding to pH, the paralleled aliquots of CDs (each for 100 μL, 1 mg/mL) were separately dissolved with 1 mL of ultrapure water, and then HCl (0.1 mol/L) and NaOH (0.1 mol/L) were used to adjust their pH (2–4.08) and pH (11.21–13.33). Accordingly, the corresponding fluorescence intensities were measured at room temperature.

In brief, 100 μL of CDs (1 mg/mL) and 200 μL of NO2 − solution (10−2 mol/L) were successively added into 1.5 mL of eppendorf (EP) tube, and diluted to 1 mL with BR buffer (pH = 10). Then, the mixture was incubated at 30 °C for 20 min, and its fluorescence intensity was recorded with the excitation of 360 nm. During the procedure of testing the selectivity of CDs for NO2 −, 200 μL of different anions including S2O8 2−, Cl−, HCO3 −, SCN−, CNS−, HPO4 2−, H2PO4 −, SO4 2− (10−2 mol/L for each) were separately added into 100 μL of CDs followed by the dilution with BR buffer (pH = 10) to 1 mL, and thus their fluorescence of the mixed solutions was measured, respectively.

Similarly, 100 μL of CDs (1 mg/mL), 200 μL of nitrite (10−2 mol/L), BR buffer (pH = 10) and 200 μL of ascorbic acid (10−2 mol/L) were successively added into 1.5 mL of Eppendorf (EP) tube. Next, the pervious solution was thoroughly mixed by vortex and subjected to the water bath at 30 °C for 20 min, and their related fluorescence intensity was measured. For studying the selectivity of this proposed approach, several analogue including GSH, EDTA, nicotinamide, melamine, aspirin, lidocaine, glucose, pyridoxine (10−2 mol/L for each) were followed the similar steps of assaying AA.

To identify the as-synthesized CDs, we first acquired their fluorescence spectra. Briefly, CDs showed the maximum emission of 441 nm excited at 360 nm (Fig. 2 A), and the blue fluorescence of CDs with UV light was observed as well as being transparent under daylight (Fig. 2A, photograph a and b). Simultaneously, there appeared an obvious peak of around 262 nm for UV–vis spectrum of CDs (Fig. 2A), which was resulted from the π-π ∗ transitions of CO. Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity exhibited the evident variations with different excitations from 320 to 380 nm, indicating that their fluorescent emissions depended on the excitation wavelengths, and the optimized was determined to be 360 nm (Fig. 2B). Also, the fluorescence lifetime of CDs was calculated to be 20.39 ns (Fig. 2C). Furthermore, the HR-TEM images of CDs described that their lattice was 0.16 nm (Fig. 2D), and these CDs were dispersed and existed in an average diameter-range of 3–5 nm by DLS (Fig. 2E), indicating the general agreement with the result of HR-TEM. In addition, there existed a peak centered at 25° for the XRD pattern of CDs (Fig. S1, ESI†), revealing the graphite-like structure of CDs due to the highly disordered carbon atoms [24,25].

To further elucidate the components and surface functional groups of CDs, their corresponding FTIR and XPS spectra were subsequently performed. In particular, the FTIR peaks at 1404 cm−1 and 1643 cm−1 were due to the stretching vibrations of CO and C–N, while the bimodal peak at 3415 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of –NH, indicating the existence of the amide group. And the peaks at 1643 cm−1 and 2371 cm−1 were ascribed to the stretching vibrations of CO and –OH from the carboxyl group. Again, the peak of 3240 cm−1 provided the evidence for the free hydroxyl group (Fig. 2F). Furthermore, their XPS survey of CDs indicated that the elements of C, N and O were the major components, and the contents of C, N and O were separately determined to be 63.03%, 16.43% and 20.54% (Fig. 3 A). To be more specific, C1s peak could be deconvoluted into three peaks of 284.70, 285.98 and 288.34 eV, which were originated from C–C/CC, C–N and CO, respectively (Fig. 3B). Also, the de-convolution of O1s spectrum (Fig. 3C) described that there existed four types of carbon bonds including COOH/CO (531.19 eV), C–OH (532.03 eV), O–C–O (532.94 eV) and C–O (533.95 eV). Again, N1s spectrum exhibited the signals of CN-C at 389.90 eV, C-N at 399.60 eV and N–H at 400.50 eV (Fig. 3D). These findings suggested that the results of FTIR and XPS spectra were consistent, revealing that there existed the groups of hydroxyl, carboxyl and amide on their surfaces of CDs.

To examine the quantum yield of CDs, their absorbance with the excitation of 360 nm was determined to be less than 0.05 (Fig. 3E and 3F), and thus the quantum yield of CDs was calculated through the following equation while quinine sulfate served as the standard [26]:
ϕ C = ϕ s
A c × P s × ( η C ) 2
A s × P C × ( η S ) 2
Hereby, A represented the integrated fluorescence intensity of CDs with the optimized excitation wavelength as well as P standing for the corresponding absorbance, and η denoted the refractive index of solutions. Accordingly, the quantum yield of CDs was calculated to be 11.83%, revealing a generally satisfactory quantum yield compared with that of the previous reports (Table S1).

To optimize the conditions of synthesizing CDs, a series of experiments were designed and performed. As revealed in Fig. S2A, B and C, ESI†, the fluorescence intensity of CDs varied accompanied by the ratio of reactants, reaction temperature and time, demonstrating that preparing CDs was regulated by these selected conditions. Consequently, the mass ratio of 1:2 for nicotinic acid and folic acid, 180 °C and 2 h were identified as the optimal conditions towards preparing CDs.

To explore the stability of CDs, the potential effect on CDs by time, temperature, concentrations of NaCl, pH, organic solvents and metal ions were therefore investigated in detail. Particularly,the CDs described here were generally stable with the time passing, varied temperature, different concentrations of NaCl and metal ions (Fig. S3A, B, C and F, ESI†), demonstrating their satisfactory stability of CDs. In contrast, the fluorescence of CDs showed response to diverse organic solvents and pH values, revealing that these factors could affect the fluorescent property of CDs (Fig. S3D and E, ESI†).

During the process of studying the stability of CDs towards different pH, their fluorescence signal showed the regular response with different pH values. In brief, the further results described that the fluorescence of CDs responded to pH variation, and increased by degrees in the pH range of 2.00 to 4.08, and its coefficient was 0.990 (Fig. 4 A and C). Meanwhile, the visual description for the fluorescence variations of CDs in acidic conditions with daylight and UV light were recorded (Fig. 4B), agreeing with the data of the fluorescent spectra of CDs in Fig. 4A. In contrast, the fluorescence intensity of CDs decreased linearly with a coefficient of 0.990 when pH value changed from 11.21 to 13.22 (Fig. 4D and F). Also, their fluorescence change of CDs in alkaline conditions was consistent with that of their fluorescent spectra (Fig. 4E). Thereby, these findings provided the evidence for their potential applications of CDs sensing pH. To further understand the mechanism of CDs sensing pH, their Zeta potentials of CDs were determined in acidic, neutral, and alkaline conditions. As indicated, the Zeta potentials of CDs gradually decreased from +10.3 mV to −11.1 mV along with pH increasing (Fig. S4A, ESI†), which was due to the protonation and deprotonation of the functional groups on their surface of CDs such as amino and carboxyl groups, thus resulting in the response of CDs towards various pH [27,28].

By virtue of the favorable fluorescence of CDs, we therefore explored their potentiality for assaying targets. Also, monitoring the amount of nitrite is considered to be valuable. Thus accordingly, two concentrations of nitrite (0.2 and 0.8 mmol/L) were separately introduced into the CDs (1 mg/mL). Importantly, the fluorescence intensity of CDs at 360 nm showed 65% decrease by adding 0.8 mmol/L NO2 − (Fig. 5 A), and further introducing ascorbic acid recovered their fluorescence of CDs (Fig. 5D), revealing that the current CDs exhibited the prospect of assaying both NO2 − and AA.

To elucidate the selectivity of CDs detecting NO2 −, NO3 −, S2O8 2−, Cl−, HCO3 −, SCN−, CNS−, CO3 2−, HPO4 2−, H2PO4 −, SO4 2− were investigated. Particularly, the results described that these negative ions exhibited scarce effect on the fluorescence of CDs (Fig. S4B, ESI†), indicating the acceptable selectivity of CDs towards NO2 −. In addition, the fluorescence of CDs with NO2 − hardly changed by introducing GSH, EDTA, nicotinamide, acetaminophen, melamine, aspirin, lidocaine, glucose or pyridoxine except ascorbic acid, demonstrating that CDs along with NO2 − could selectively assay AA (Fig. S4C, ESI†).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimizing the conditions of CDs detecting NO2 − and AA
ce:section.ce:para: To optimize the conditions of CDs detecting NO2 −, a variety of factors that may affect this detection were investigated. Since the fluorescence of CDs was sensitive to acid-base environment, different pH values were initially introduced into the processes of CDs sensing NO2 −. In this regard, the fluorescence decrease of CDs reached the maximum in presence of NO2 − at pH = 10 (Fig. S5A, ESI†). Meanwhile, the possible influence of temperature and time on detecting NO2 − was also systematically explored (Fig. S5B and C, ESI†). Accordingly, pH = 10.0, 50 °C and 20 min were identified as the optimized conditions during the procedures of CDs detecting NO2 −. Subsequently, the similar experiments were designed to obtain the optimal conditions for CDs assaying ascorbic acid. In brief, their fluorescence intensity of CDs varied along with different reaction time and temperature, indicating that assaying AA depended on the selected conditions (Fig. S6A and B, ESI†). Thereby, we confirmed that the conditions of 60 min and 30 °C were optimal for CDs sensing AA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishing the detections of nitrite and AA
ce:section.ce:para: By utilizing the optimized conditions that were previously identified, the strategy of detecting both nitrite and AA was thus successfully established. Briefly, various concentrations of nitrite (0.008 to 0.8 mmol/L) were separately added into CDs under the optimized conditions, and their fluorescence of CDs regularly decreased with the increasing amount of nitrite (R2 = 0.995), and the corresponding detection limit was 21.2 µM with a signal-to-noise ratio of 3 (Fig. 5B and C). More importantly, their quenched fluorescence of CDs gradually recovered by introducing ascorbic acid, and the fluorescence variation exhibited an acceptable linear relationship with the concentration of AA from 0.0001 to 0.8 mmol as well as the detection limit of 50 µM (Fig. 5E and F). Therefore, the current CDs provided a new way of detecting NO2 − and AA. Moreover, we compared the current strategy with the already reported detections on NO2 − and AA (Tables S2 and S3), demonstrating its generally satisfactory detection limit and linear range.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of CDs detecting NO2 − and AA
ce:section.ce:para: The mechanism of fluorescence quenching is mainly associated with static quenching effect (SQE), dynamic quenching effect (DQE), Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), photoinduced electron transfer (PET) and inner filter effect (IFE) [29].To understand the mechanism for CDs assaying NO2 −, we first compared the UV–vis absorption of NO2 − (a), their excitation (b) and emission (c) of CDs (Fig. 6 A). In brief, no UV–vis adsorption peak for NO2 − from 250 to 600 nm was observed, and its absorption did not overlap with the excitation and emission of CDs, revealing that the quenched fluorescence of CDs by NO2 − is not regulated by FRET or IFE.

We thereby addressed whether the fluorescence quenching of CDs was resulted from the DQE or SQE [30]. Both DQE and SQE could be theoretically described by Stern-Volmer equation [30]:
F 0
F
- 1 = K SV
[ Q ]
where, [Q] is the concentration of NO2 −, and F0 and F separately represent the fluorescence intensity of CDs in absence and presence of NO2 −, and
K SV
is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant [30]. In this study, F0/F showed the linearity with different concentrations of NO2 −, and the linear equation was fitted as the follows: F/F0 = 2.0824 [C] + 1.0449. For DQE,
K SV = K q × τ 0
, where,
K q
is the molecular quenching rate constant as well as τ0 for the fluorescence lifetime. For CDs,
K SV
= 2.0824 mM−1 with τ0 = 20.31 ns.
K q
was calculated to be 1.02 × 1011 M−1 s−1, Accordingly, this value was higher than the typical fluorescence molecular quenching rate constant of 1 × 1010 M−1 s−1 for DQE [31]. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching was mainly resulted from SQE rather than DQE. Meanwhile, SQE usually refers to the formation of the non-fluorescence complex through the interaction between CDs and the quenchers. More detailed, forming new complex could lead to the absorption spectrum of CDs changed. As shown in Fig. 6B, the new absorption peaks appeared at 210 nm, 265 nm and 360 nm by introducing NO2 −, describing that new complex emerged. In addition, the FTIR data of CDs with NO2 − showed the stretching vibrations at 1001 cm−1, 1060 cm−1, and 1425 cm−1, which was due to the new complex forming (Fig. 6C). These findings suggested CDs interacted with NO2 − and formed the non-fluorescent complex, and thus supporting the occurrence of SQE. In contrast, changed FTIR and UV–vis adsorption peaks of CDs with both nitrite and AA provided the evidence for part of nitrite consumed by AA, and inhibiting the fluorescence quenching of CDs (Fig. 6C and D). In particular, the peaks of 1425 cm−1, 1060 cm−1 and 1001 cm−1 provided the evidence for the formation of the complex between CDs and NO2 −, whereas the disappearance of the peak at 1425 cm−1 after the addition of AA indicated the destruction of this complex. Again, the enhancement of 1637 cm−1 peak described the stretching vibration of C-N bond, which was induced by the destroyed complex. In addition, the size of CDs with NO2 − increased compared with that of CDs only, and the further introduction of AA resulted in the decreased size, demonstrating that AA destroyed the complex of CDs and NO2 − and confirming the results by FTIR (Fig. S7, ESI†). Taken together, the lone pair electrons of the amide groups on the surfaces of CDs interacted with NO2 − and formed the metastable complex, and the further introduction of AA destroyed the complex, and thus recovering their fluorescence of CDs (Fig. 6E).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of NO2 − and AA in real sample
ce:section.ce:para: To validate the practicality of this sensing strategy, the lake-water samples supplemented with NO2 − and AA were studied. Briefly, these experimental results demonstrated the practical value of the current method, and may broaden the ways of detecting NO2 − and AA in actual samples (Tables 1 and 2 ).

In summary, we here raised one type of CDs through a favorable method, while both nicotinic acid and folic acid played the role as the carbon source. Meanwhile, the proposed CDs exhibited the generally favorable blue-fluorescence, and were elucidated to be equipped with the functional groups of amide and carboxyl. Besides, their fluorescence of the present CDs responded to varying pH in accordance with a linear relationship, demonstrating their potential application of sensing pH. Significantly, the introduction of NO2 − into CDs resulted in their reduced fluorescence, and further adding ascorbic acid could recover the fluorescence of CDs, providing a new approach of detecting NO2 − and AA. In this regard, we thereby achieved the selective detections of NO2 − and ascorbic acid, and their corresponding linear ranges were 0.008–0.8 mmol and 0.0001 to 0.8 mmol, respectively. Again, our findings supported the mechanism of CDs sensing NO2 − as the static quenching effect, that was resulted from the complex formation between CDs and NO2 −. Moreover, the further introduction of ascorbic acid prevented the complex formation, and thus recovering their fluorescence intensity of CDs. Again, the CDs described here may broaden the avenues of detecting NO2 − and ascorbic acid.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

Lanlan Gan: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Qian Su: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Zhangbao Chen: Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Xiaoming Yang: Supervision, Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support by Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing (cstc2018jcyjAX0197).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2020.147269.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Cutting sp2clusters in graphene sheets into colloidal graphene quantum dots with strong green fluorescence
J. Mater. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Pan, L. Guo, J. Zhang, C. Xi, Q. Xue, H. Huang, J. Li, Cutting sp2clusters in graphene sheets into colloidal graphene quantum dots with strong green fluorescence, J. Mater. Chem. 22 (2012) 3314-3318.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A surfactant-assisted redox hydrothermal route to prepare highly photoluminescent carbon quantum dots with aggregation-induced emission enhancement properties
Chem. Commun.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Gao, C. Liu, Z. Wu, Q. Zeng, X. Yang, W. Wu, Y. Li, C. Huang, A surfactant-assisted redox hydrothermal route to prepare highly photoluminescent carbon quantum dots with aggregation-induced emission enhancement properties, Chem. Commun. 49 (2013) 8015-8017.
